sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Altered level of consciousness (ALOC) refers to any change from a patient’s baseline alertness, orientation, or responsiveness. It may range from mild confusion to complete unresponsiveness (coma). ALOC is not a diagnosis but a symptom of an underlying condition which can include metabolic disturbances (hypo/hyperglycaemia), hypoxia, head injuries, CVA/TIA, seizures, intoxication (drugs, alcohol), infection, shock or toxins.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reduced alertness or responsiveness, patient may be confused, dizzy or light-headed, drowsy, or unrousable
- Disorientated with inability to recognise time, place, or people
- Abnormal behaviour, anxious, restless, sleepy, sleep like state or comatose
- Inability to follow commands, may be unable to respond to verbal or painful stimuli
- Abnormal motor responses and may include posturing, seizures, or flaccidity
- Pupillary abnormalities, abnormal breathing patterns, or evidence of trauma
- Pallor, sweaty, nausea, palpitations
- Full recovery after a few minutes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The differential for ALOC is broad and often time critical
- Consider intracranial causes such as stroke, intracranial haemorrhage, CNS infection, post-ictal state, status epilepticus, or hypertensive encephalopathy
- Consider extra-cranial causes such as dysrhythmia, blood glucose abnormality, electrolyte disturbance, toxic exposure, hypoxia, sepsis, shock, environmental exposure, or substance withdrawal
- Rapid improvement after simple treatment does not remove the need to consider serious underlying pathology

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety and protect the patient from further harm
- Primary Survey
- Position for airway protection and apply spinal motion restriction only where clinically indicated
- Administer oxygen if hypoxaemic or in respiratory distress
- Record a full vital sign survey including blood glucose, temperature, GCS or AVPU, and pupils
- Manage immediately reversible causes such as hypoglycaemia, hypoxia, opioid toxicity, arrhythmia, or environmental exposure where evident
- Perform focused neurological assessment, including FAST where appropriate, without delaying transport
- Seek early senior support if the cause remains unclear, the patient does not improve promptly, or deterioration continues

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management if airway reflexes are lost or ventilation is inadequate
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG where cardiac, toxic, metabolic, or syncope-related causes are possible
- Gain vascular access if medication, fluids, or further investigation may be required
- Consider point-of-care blood testing if it will inform immediate management without delaying transport
- Consider IV fluids if hypovolaemia, sepsis, or shock is suspected
- Consider IV naloxone titrated to effect in suspected opioid toxicity where advanced management is required
- Treat seizure activity, dysrhythmia, or other identified causes in line with the relevant condition-specific guideline

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - ALOC may fluctuate with time and response to treatment
